topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell rang, a jarring intrusion in the quiet afternoon. I peered through the peephole, my heart giving a sudden, frantic thump against my ribs. A delivery person stood on the porch, holding a medium-sized cardboard box addressed to the previous occupants of Apartment 3B. My hands started to tremble as I fumbled with the lock, the metal cool and slick under my palms. When I finally managed to open the door, the scent of old cardboard and something faintly metallic wafted out. Inside was a collection of dusty books and a single, faded photograph. I stood frozen on the threshold, the sheer volume of unexpected responsibility settling on my shoulders like a heavy, suffocating blanket. I backed away slowly, my gaze darting from the package to the hallway, suddenly unable to process the next move.

[story 2]

It arrived just as I was trying to settle into the relative calm of the empty apartment. A delivery notice confirmed it: a parcel meant for the unit upstairs. When I retrieved the box, it felt disproportionately heavy, the tape brittle under my fingers. Opening it revealed a haphazard arrangement of forgotten items—a chipped ceramic mug, a stack of old mail, and a handwritten note in looping, unfamiliar script. I set the box down on the entryway table, and immediately, a tightness began to constrict in my chest. I paced three steps to the window, breathing in shallow, quick gasps. Everything felt too much; the silence of the space suddenly amplified the buzzing anxiety in my head, making the simple act of unpacking feel monumental and impossible.

[story 3]

I hadn't expected anything. Just a package, delivered to my door, addressed to the unit above mine. When I finally wrestled the box inside, the weight of it seemed to press down on me, making my knees feel weak. Inside, there were boxes of mismatched linens and some old, brittle documents. My jaw tightened, and I found myself running a hand repeatedly through my hair, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. I tried to focus on the task—sorting, storing, figuring out what to do with it—but the sheer multitude of things staring back at me created a fog in my vision. Every corner of the room seemed to hold a potential crisis, and I just stood there, rooted to the spot, unable to summon the energy to begin.
